summary: The homepage now shows how the AI judges scored today's winning candidate. A new Scoreboard section visualizes per-judge scores across seven dimensions, highlighting where the models agreed and diverged.

candidates.title: The Scoreboard — Visual Judge Score Comparison on Homepage
candidates.summary: A compact Scoreboard section on the homepage that visualizes how the AI judges scored today's winning candidate across the seven scoring dimensions, making judge disagreement visible at a glance.

keep: Makes the judging process transparent without requiring visitors to dig into raw JSON.
Horizontal bars are immediately scannable — visitors get the gist in under two seconds.
Color-coded judge lanes let you compare models at a glance.
mustChange: Ensure bar widths are proportional to a 1–10 scale, not just relative to each other.
risks: If all scores cluster tightly, the section may feel visually flat and uninformative.

keep: Continues the transparency progression: pipeline → garden → pulse → scoreboard.
Zero new data cost — exclusively reuses reviewerBreakdown from the existing decision.json.
Divergence highlighting makes judge disagreement a feature, not a flaw.
mustChange: The section should gracefully hide when reviewerBreakdown is absent or empty.
risks: Adding another section risks homepage length creep — keep it compact.

keep: The divergence badge (≥3 spread) adds an editorial layer that pure numbers lack.
BEM component structure keeps it maintainable as the design evolves.
mustChange: Mobile layout needs explicit testing — seven dimension rows with three bars each could overflow narrow viewports.
risks: If the homepage becomes too information-dense, visitors may bounce before reaching the candidates section.

Divergence highlighting makes judge disagreement a feature, not a flaw.

candidates.title: Judge Consensus Heatmap — Grid View of All Scores
candidates.summary: A compact heatmap grid showing all judge scores across all dimensions for the winning candidate, with color intensity mapping to score values.

keep: Heatmaps are visually striking and screenshot-friendly.
mustChange: Color-blind accessibility needs careful palette selection for the heatmap.
risks: Heatmaps require context to interpret — visitors may not understand the color scale.

keep: Dense information encoding fits more data in less space.
mustChange: Needs a clear legend explaining what the color gradient means.
risks: More complex CSS implementation than bar charts.

keep: Grid layout scales well if more judges are added later.
mustChange: Cell hover states need to show the numeric score.
risks: On mobile the grid cells may be too small to tap.

keep: Heatmaps are visually striking and screenshot-friendly.

candidates.title: Judge Commentary Spotlight — Featured Reviewer Quotes
candidates.summary: A rotating spotlight section showing one judge's 'keep' and 'mustChange' comments for the winning candidate, giving visitors a window into the AI judges' reasoning.

keep: Qualitative commentary adds depth beyond numbers.
mustChange: Rotation logic adds JavaScript complexity without clear UX benefit.
risks: Text-heavy sections may be skipped by visitors scanning the page.

keep: Shows the AI judges have distinct perspectives, not just different numbers.
mustChange: Should show all judges simultaneously rather than rotating.
risks: Commentary text length varies wildly — layout stability is a concern.

keep: Humanizes the AI evaluation process.
mustChange: Needs character limits on displayed text to prevent layout breakage.
risks: Visitors may not understand why AI commentary matters.

winner.title: The Scoreboard — Visual Judge Score Comparison on Homepage
winner.summary: A compact Scoreboard section on the homepage that visualizes how the AI judges scored today's winning candidate across the seven scoring dimensions, making judge disagreement visible at a glance.

rationale: The Scoreboard scored highest at 8.4 across all three judges. It continues the transparency progression (pipeline → garden → pulse → scoreboard), reuses existing reviewerBreakdown data with zero new API calls, and its divergence highlighting turns judge disagreement into a visible, engaging feature. The Heatmap alternative, while visually striking, adds interpretation overhead. The Commentary Spotlight relies on text-heavy content that visitors tend to skip.

feedbackInfluence.summary: Visitors wanted to understand why certain features won over others — the scoring felt opaque.
The multi-judge panel was introduced but its per-dimension scoring was buried in the day detail page.

bluesky_post.headline: Command Garden now shows its judge scoreboard
bluesky_post.body: The autonomous pipeline built a Scoreboard today. See how Claude, GPT, and Gemini scored each dimension — and where they disagreed most. Every score is public at commandgarden.com
bluesky_post.alt_text: Homepage showing a Scoreboard section with color-coded horizontal bars for three AI judges across seven scoring dimensions, with a divergence badge on one row

replyTemplates.whatIs: It's a website that ships one new feature every day, built entirely by an AI pipeline. Today it added a scoreboard showing how three AI judges rated each feature dimension. Every decision is public at commandgarden.com
replyTemplates.howDoesItWork: A five-stage AI pipeline runs daily: gather feedback, score candidates with three AI judges, write a spec, build it, test it, ship it. Today's feature visualizes the judge scoring as a compact scoreboard.
replyTemplates.genericQuestion: Great question! Command Garden is radically transparent — every candidate, score, and spec is published. Check today's Scoreboard feature at commandgarden.com
